Logic Pro X Interface.description: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, confirmed by a male speaker in Hindi as 'Logic Pro' and referred to as its 'arrangement', showing a newly created software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1', with a channel strip containing EQ, MIDI FX, and volume controls, and an empty timeline ready for use.

DJ NEO Logo Animation.description: A dark, particle-filled animation featuring a gold 'DJ NEO' logo with the tagline 'LEARN ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, representing the channel of the speaker, DJ Neo, who welcomes viewers and introduces the video's topic.

New Track Dialog Box.description: A pop-up window in Logic Pro X with tabs for 'Software Instrument', 'Audio', and 'Drummer', currently set to 'Software Instrument' with 'Empty Channel Strip' selected. A male speaker in Hindi details the options presented, including 'software instrument, audio, drummer, external MIDI, guitar aur bass', and explains the choice between 'empty channel strip' and 'default patch', stating his preference for the former before the 'Create' button is clicked to finalize track creation.

Intro Title Screen.description: A black screen with bold red and white text reading 'LOGIC PRO INTRO CLASS #CLASS-1', serving as a title card for the tutorial whose purpose is explained by DJ Neo in the accompanying audio.

raw_segments: The video opens with an upbeat electronic music track playing over a dark-themed Logic Pro X interface, displaying two software instrument tracks, a Loop Browser, and transport controls set to 120 BPM. The music continues as the scene transitions to a stylized, dark, particle-filled animation featuring a gold 'DJ NEO' logo with the tagline 'LEARN ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, before the music fades out with a synth flourish and a whoosh sound effect. A bold black screen then appears, presenting the title 'LOGIC PRO INTRO CLASS #CLASS-1' in red and white text. A male voice, identified as DJ Neo, then greets viewers in Hindi, welcoming them to his channel and explaining that after a long absence, he is fulfilling requests to create introductory videos on Logic Pro X, which he describes as a "बहुत ही बेहतरीन डिजिटल ऑडियो वर्कस्टेशन" (very excellent Digital Audio Workstation). As he speaks, the video returns to the Logic Pro X interface, where a 'New Track' dialog box is open, showing the 'Software Instrument' tab selected with 'Empty Channel Strip' chosen and the 'Create' button highlighted, setting the stage for the instructional content as DJ Neo continues to explain that this video will provide an overview of Logic Pro and how to work with it.

The video opens with the dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, where a New Track Dialog Box is prominently displayed. A male voice, speaking in Hindi, introduces the software, stating, "abhi Logic Pro hai... main already Logic Pro khol ke rakha hoon," and then details the options available in the dialog box, mentioning "software instrument, audio, drummer, external MIDI, guitar aur bass," which are visible as tabs. The dialog box is set to 'Software Instrument' with 'Empty Channel Strip' selected. The speaker elaborates on the 'Instrument' dropdown, noting that one can choose an "empty channel strip ya aap ek default patch bhi le sakte ho," and explains his preference, "main hamesha empty channel strip leta hoon kyunki mujhe isme zyada comfort lagta hai." As he speaks about other instrument options like "keyboard ya kuch bhi aap ya synthesizer," the user visually scrolls through the 'Instrument' dropdown menu, revealing options such as 'Alchemy', 'Drum Synth', 'ES1', and 'Vintage Electric Piano'. After reviewing, the user re-selects 'Empty Channel Strip'. The speaker then announces his intention to create a track, saying, "to abhi chalte hain hum log ek track banate hain, mujhe abhi ek track banana hai," and instructs, "create software instrument track." The user clicks the 'Create' button, closing the dialog box. The speaker confirms the action, "to hamara aap dekh sakte ho ki idhar ek instrument track create ho chuka hai," as a new software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1' appears on the interface. The interface now shows the newly created track with its channel strip, including controls for EQ, MIDI FX, and volume, while the main timeline remains empty, ready for recording or editing, which the speaker refers to as "yeh Logic Pro ka arrangement hai."

event_blocks.summary: The Logic Pro X Interface, presented in a dark theme, initially shows a single software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1' with an empty timeline. A male speaker, speaking in Hindi, draws parallels to FL Studio regarding track creation and layering. As a second track, 'Inst 2', is added, creating two parallel tracks, the speaker highlights the 'inspector button' and explains that each track gets its own 'mixer window', demonstrating this by selecting 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' to show their respective 'channel strips' feeding into a 'stereo output'. The user toggles the record enable buttons on both tracks, then navigates to the 'Logic Pro' menu and selects 'About Logic Pro', where the speaker corrects the interface name to 'Logic Pro' and points out the version as 10.6.1. After closing this window, the user clicks the time display in the control bar, which the speaker identifies as the 'transport window' with customizable display options. The user selects 'Beats & Time (Large)', changing the time display to '01:00:00:00.00' in a larger font. The speaker then points out the metronome and countdown timer buttons, and the loop function. Upon clicking the play button, a metronome click sound is heard, and the playhead moves across the empty timeline as the speaker explains how to disable the metronome and loop, and mentions the 'Enter' key shortcut to return the playhead to the start. After stopping playback, the user navigates to the mixer view by clicking the 'Mix' button (or using the 'X' shortcut), revealing channel strips for 'Inst 1', 'Inst 2', 'Stereo Out', and 'Master', each with faders and pan controls. The speaker clarifies that 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' are the created tracks, 'Stereo Out' is for mixing and mastering plugins, leading to the 'Master' output. The user returns to the Tracks view by pressing 'X' again. The speaker then introduces the Piano Roll, which opens by pressing 'P' or clicking the 'Inst 1' track header, revealing an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions. The user hovers over editing options like 'Time Quantize' and 'Velocity'. The speaker clarifies active windows by their blue highlighting and demonstrates keyboard shortcuts (Command + arrow keys) for zooming within the interface, specifically for the Piano Roll editor. Throughout these interactions, the timeline remains empty, the control bar consistently displays 4/4 time signature and 120 BPM tempo, and the top menu bar shows 'Logic Pro', 'Untitled - Tracks', with the system time progressing.

Logic Pro X Interface.description: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, featuring two software instrument tracks 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' with record enable buttons. The speaker demonstrates how to open and close the Mixer window, open the Piano Roll editor (by pressing 'P' or clicking the track header), identify active windows via blue highlighting, and use Command + arrow key shortcuts for zooming in and out across the interface, including within the Piano Roll. The timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format.

raw_segments: The video opens on a dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, initially displaying a single software instrument track labeled 'Inst 1' with an empty timeline. A male speaker, speaking in Hindi, begins by explaining how tracks are created and layered in digital audio workstations like FL Studio, drawing parallels to the current interface. As the user adds a second track, 'Inst 2', creating two parallel tracks, the speaker highlights the 'inspector button' on the left side of the interface and explains that each track, when created, gets its own 'mixer window'. He demonstrates this by selecting 'Inst 1' and then 'Inst 2', showing how their respective 'channel strips' appear, ultimately feeding into a 'stereo output'. The user then toggles the record enable buttons on both tracks. Subsequently, the user navigates to the 'Logic Pro' menu and selects 'About Logic Pro'. The speaker, referring to the interface, corrects himself from 'Logic Pro X' to 'Logic Pro' and explicitly points out the displayed version as 10.6.1 in the pop-up window. After closing this window, the user clicks on the time display in the control bar. The speaker continues, referring to this area as the 'transport window', explaining that the display can be customized. The user then selects 'Beats & Time (Large)' from the dropdown, changing the time display to '01:00:00:00.00' in a larger font, aligning with the speaker's explanation of display customization.

The video opens with the Logic Pro X Interface displayed in its default dark theme, showing two software instrument tracks labeled 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with their record enable buttons being toggled on and off. A male voice-over begins, pointing out the time display, which the user then changes from 'Beats & Project (Large)' to 'Beats & Time (Large)' via the control bar. The speaker continues, identifying the metronome and countdown timer buttons, and then the loop function. As the user clicks the play button, a metronome click sound begins, and the playhead moves across the empty timeline, while the speaker explains how to turn off the metronome and loop, and mentions the 'Enter' key shortcut to return the playhead to the start, calling it his favorite function. After stopping playback, the user navigates to the mixer view by clicking the 'Mix' button, which the speaker explains can also be accessed via the 'X' shortcut in Logic Pro. The mixer reveals channel strips for 'Inst 1', 'Inst 2', 'Stereo Out', and 'Master', each with faders, pan controls, and automation settings. The speaker elaborates that 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' are the two tracks created, and the 'Stereo Out' is for mixing and mastering plugins, leading to the 'Master' output. The user briefly hovers over the 'Inst 1' channel strip before returning to the Tracks view, prompted by the speaker's mention of pressing 'X' again. Throughout the segment, the interface remains static with no audio or MIDI regions added, the control bar consistently displays the time signature as 4/4 and tempo as 120 BPM, and the top menu bar shows 'Logic Pro', 'Untitled - Tracks', and the system time progressing from 'Sun 14 Feb 19:59' to '20:00'.

The video opens with a user interacting with the dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, where a speaker explains that the Mixer window can be made to disappear and then reopened by navigating to the 'Window' menu and selecting 'Open Mixer'. The speaker notes that this Mixer window, displaying vertical channel strips for 'Inst 1', 'Inst 2', 'Stereo Out', and 'Master' with faders and controls, opens separately and can be moved to a dual screen setup. After the user closes the Mixer and returns to the main Tracks view, the speaker introduces the Piano Roll, stating it opens by pressing 'P' (though visually the user clicks the 'Inst 1' track header to reveal it at the bottom). The empty Piano Roll grid, with piano keys and time divisions, is then explored by the user, hovering over editing options like 'Time Quantize' and 'Velocity'. The speaker further clarifies how to identify active windows by their blue highlighting and demonstrates keyboard shortcuts (Command + arrow keys) for zooming in and out within the interface, specifically mentioning their application for the Piano Roll editor. Throughout this, the timeline remains empty, and the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format, indicating no recording or playback.

event_blocks.summary: The Logic Pro X Interface, presented in a dark theme, initially displays two software instrument tracks, 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with their record enable buttons being toggled. The Piano Roll editor for 'Inst 1' is closed, and the control bar is set to 'Beats & Time (Large)'. The Loop Browser is introduced, showcasing Logic Pro's extensive loop collection categorized by instruments and genres, with loops like '12 String Dream 01' and 'Irish Poly Harp 01' being previewed and adapting to the project's tempo. The Note Pad is opened under the 'Project' tab, where 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' and 'Key Signature: C' are typed, with the sound of typing audible. The key signature is then changed to 'E major' and subsequently 'A major' via the control bar dropdown, and the tempo is set to '120', resulting in a blue tempo region on the timeline. The video transitions to loading the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' onto 'Inst 1' after navigating the Library panel. The Mixer view reveals channel strips with effects like 'Channel EQ' and 'Tape Delay', and bus sends for auxiliary channels are adjusted. Reverb is applied, and an empty MIDI region is created on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, extended to four bars to form a chord progression in C major. Notes C, E, and G are placed and adjusted in the Piano Roll editor, with Time Quantize set to 1/16 Note and Velocity at 80. A new software instrument track, 'Inst 2', is created and assigned 'Studio Strings', with the 'Studio Strings Plugin Interface' displaying controls for articulation, dynamics, and volume, and 'Violins 1' selected. The MIDI region from the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track is copied and pasted to the 'Strings' track, which is then renamed. The 'Strings' track's volume fader is adjusted in the Mixer View, and a string melody accompanied by piano begins to play, demonstrating the audio output.

Logic Pro X Interface.description: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface showing 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks, both with record enable buttons toggled. A green MIDI region is created and extended to 4 bars. The Piano Roll editor is opened for 'Yamaha Grand Piano', displaying an empty grid with piano keys and time divisions, along with editing parameters. The control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)'. The interface is where the speaker demonstrates creating and managing instruments, with his voice guiding the actions in Hindi, and it produces the string melody and piano accompaniment heard during playback.

Loop Browser.description: A panel on the right side of the Logic Pro X interface that displays a list of available audio loops categorized by instrument, genre, and descriptors. The speaker explains that loops will play according to the selected key. The user scrolls through loops like '12 String Dream 01' and 'Windy City Piano', highlighting them for preview.

Note Pad.description: A text input area within the Logic Pro X interface, accessed via the 'Project' tab. Visually, it shows typed text like 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' and 'Key Signature: C'. The male speaker, speaking in Hindi, describes it as a 'very good option' for writing down project details to aid memory, stating, 'whatever details you have about your project, you can write them here.'

Intro Title Screen.description: A black screen with bold red and white text reading 'LOGIC PRO INTRO CLASS #CLASS-1', serving as a title card for the tutorial.

Mixer View.description: The Mixer interface in Logic Pro X showing multiple channel strips. The 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track is visible, and the user adjusts the volume fader for the 'Strings' track, significantly reducing it, while the speaker's voice explains the process and the resulting change in the audio output.

DJ NEO Logo Animation.description: A dark, particle-filled animation featuring a gold 'DJ NEO' logo with the tagline 'LEARN ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button at the bottom, appearing briefly during the speaker's narration.

Studio Strings Plugin Interface.description: A dark-themed plugin window for 'Studio Strings' with a violin graphic, featuring controls for Articulation (set to 'Violins 1'), Dynamics via CC, Cutoff, Resonance, Volume, Attack, and Release. The speaker explicitly names it, saying "Studio Strings" and "Look, here's my Studio Strings" in Hindi, confirming its appearance and function.

New Track Dialog Box.description: A pop-up window in Logic Pro X, appearing after the speaker discusses choosing a new instrument, with tabs for 'Software Instrument', 'Audio', and 'Drummer', currently set to 'Software Instrument' with 'Empty Channel Strip' selected and the 'Create' button clicked to finalize track creation.

raw_segments: The video opens on the dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, displaying two software instrument tracks, 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2', with their record enable buttons being toggled on and off. The speaker states, "Abhi piano roll band kar dete hai," indicating the Piano Roll editor for 'Inst 1' is closed. The control bar at the top is set to 'Beats & Time (Large)' format. The speaker then introduces the Loop Browser, saying, "aur yeh hai hamara loop ke liye... idhar loop ka jo hai browser hai pura," and highlights the extensive collection of Logic Pro loops categorized by instruments and genres. The user opens the Loop Browser, navigating the 'Instrument' tab and scrolling through various loops like '12 String Dream 01' and '12 String Dream 02', highlighting them for preview. A loop plays, which the speaker explains, "ye jo loop hai aapka tempo ke hisaab se ye khel raha abhi because ye project ka tempo leke ye loop khelta hai," adapting to the project's tempo. The user continues scrolling past 'Windy City Piano' and 'Wonderland Strings' before closing the browser, with the speaker emphasizing the utility of loops and mentioning a future tutorial. Finally, the speaker introduces the "Note Pad" as a place for project notes, and the user opens it under the 'Project' tab, typing "Project Tempo: 120 BPM" to set the project's tempo, with the sound of typing audible. Throughout, the user interacts with the software via mouse clicks and scrolling.

The video opens with the dark-themed Logic Pro X Interface, displaying 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks with their record enable buttons being toggled. The timeline is initially empty, and the control bar is set to 'Beats & Time (Large)'. A male speaker, speaking in Hindi, confirms, "Hamara ho gaya 120 BPM" (Our 120 BPM is done) as the user navigates to the 'Project' tab and opens the Note Pad. The speaker explains, "ab jo bhi details hai aapka project ke bare mein aap idhar likh sakte ho" (whatever details you have about your project, you can write them here), praising the Note Pad as a "bahut hi behtareen option" (very good option) for remembering project specifics. The user then types 'Project Tempo: 120 BPM' and 'Key Signature: C' into the Note Pad. The speaker then states, "Key signature C major," and points out, "Ye dekhiye idhar C major hai" (See, here it is C major) as the user clicks the 'Signature' dropdown in the control bar, selecting 'E major', which updates the interface. The speaker continues to highlight other UI elements like markers and tempo, mentioning future tutorials. The user then selects '120' from the 'Tempo' dropdown, causing a blue '120' tempo region to appear on the timeline. The speaker further explains how to set the song's key, then points out "global preferences" and confirms the current settings: "C major ka key mein hai, 4/4 time signature hai" (it's in C major key, 4/4 time signature). The user subsequently changes the key signature to 'A major' via the dropdown. The segment concludes with the Piano Roll editor opening for 'Inst 1', revealing an empty grid with piano keys and various editing parameters.

The video opens with the Logic Pro X Interface, displaying 'Inst 1' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks with record enable buttons toggled. A male speaker with a Hindi accent explains that the key signature can be changed, demonstrating by selecting 'D minor' then 'C major', noting that any loops will then play according to the new key. The Loop Browser appears on the right, showing various categorized loops as the user scrolls through options like 'Irish Poly Harp 01' and 'Jack Impovy 01'. A 'DJ Neo subscribe to my channel' overlay with a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button briefly appears. The speaker then guides on loading instruments, prompting the user to open the Library panel, navigate to 'Piano', and select 'Yamaha Grand Piano'. A loading message appears, and 'Inst 1' updates to 'Yamaha Grand Piano'. The user switches to the Mixer view, revealing multiple channel strips with effects like 'Channel EQ' and 'Tape Delay'. The speaker highlights that Logic Pro has pre-configured settings, including bus sends for auxiliary channels with effects like delay, as the user adjusts the 'Bus 4' send on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, with the Master channel and 'Bnce' fader visible.

The video opens with the Logic Pro X interface, displaying 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks, both with record enable buttons toggled. A male voice speaking Hindi explains that reverb is also applied to the track, stating his preference for personal settings over presets, but demonstrating how to work with the interface. He then adjusts the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track's channel strip, including MIDI and Audio FX, and mentions the need to create an envelope to draw MIDI. Following his instruction to "right-click or two-finger click on Mac" to "create empty MIDI region," a green MIDI region labeled 'Yamaha Grand Piano' appears on the timeline. The speaker explains how to extend and loop the region, then extends it to 4 bars, stating the goal is to create a four-bar chord progression. He then double-clicks the region, causing the Piano Roll editor to open, revealing an empty grid. The speaker announces he will create a "small chord progression in C major." He then clicks to place three notes on the C3 key, identifying them as "C," "E," and "G" to form a "C major" chord, which are then selected and dragged to extend their duration. The editing panel on the left shows parameters like Time Quantize set to 1/16 Note and Velocity at 80, while the timeline displays the project in 4/4 time at 120 BPM.

The video opens with the Logic Pro X Interface, a dark-themed digital audio workstation, where a male speaker with a Hindi accent begins by describing a "one-time chord progression" as the user edits MIDI notes on a 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track in the Piano Roll editor. Piano notes are heard playing as the user drags and adjusts note positions and velocities, then uses Time Quantize settings for precise alignment. The speaker continues, mentioning "then a GBD" and "then DFA," with corresponding piano notes playing. After editing, the speaker says, "Now let's play this," and a four-bar chord progression is heard playing on the piano. The speaker confirms, "This is our four-bar chord progression that we created here." The user then navigates to the track list and creates a new software instrument track named 'Inst 2'. The speaker explains, "And I want to choose my personal instrument now," leading to the 'New Track Dialog Box' appearing, where 'Studio Strings' is selected and assigned to 'Inst 2'. The 'Studio Strings Plugin Interface' then appears, showing controls for articulation, dynamics, and volume, with 'Violins 1' selected. The speaker elaborates on instrument selection, noting that choosing from certain areas provides "totally completely raw" sounds, while other selections allow Logic Pro to add its own effects. The user switches to the 'Library' panel, browsing sound categories like 'Bass', 'Drum Kit', and 'Piano', before returning to the track view. The timeline shows the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' region extended to 4 bars, and the control bar displays the time in 'Beats & Time (Large)' format. The user interacts with the 'Mixer View', adjusting the 'Bus 4' send on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, with the Master channel visible, all within the dark-themed interface with green MIDI regions and clear labels.

The video opens on the Logic Pro X Interface, displaying 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Inst 2' software instrument tracks with their record enable buttons toggled. A male speaker's voice is heard, initially saying "Studio Strings" and "mono," then stating in Hindi, "Look, here's my Studio Strings," as a green MIDI region is created on the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track and extended to four bars. The speaker continues in Hindi, explaining, "And I'll copy-paste this same thing there," and provides instructions: "To copy, press and hold Alt or the Option key, click and drag down," as the MIDI region is copied to the 'Inst 2' track. The Piano Roll editor is opened for the 'Yamaha Grand Piano' track, showing an empty grid and editing parameters. The speaker then confirms, "And this instrument is now Strings," as 'Inst 2' is renamed 'Strings' via a context menu. The Studio Strings Plugin Interface appears, showing controls for Articulation set to 'Violins 1', Dynamics, Cutoff, Resonance, Volume, Attack, and Release, accompanied by a violin graphic. The speaker explains the origin of the MIDI, "And look here, we actually took the chords from Yamaha Grand Piano, so it will show here." He then guides the user on renaming, "If you want to change this, right-click or two-finger click, and you can rename it, Name and Color, Rename Regions, Name Regions by Tracks," as the user right-clicks the 'Strings' region to rename it. The track name in the left panel also updates to 'Strings'. The speaker confirms, "Look, this is now according to the Strings tracks." In the Mixer View, the user adjusts the volume fader for the 'Strings' track, reducing it significantly, while the control bar displays time in 'Beats & Time (Large)'. A string melody, accompanied by piano, begins to play, demonstrating the audio output, with the speaker continuing to adjust the volume fader. The speaker concludes in Hindi, "So this is what I just showed you, how we create instruments in this and basically how we can use it."

event_blocks.summary: The Logic Pro X Interface, a dark-themed digital audio workstation, is presented with 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Strings' software instrument tracks record-enabled, featuring two green four-bar MIDI regions on the timeline, a 4/4 time signature, 120 BPM, and C major key. The track mixer is visible in the left panel, with the 'Strings' track selected and its volume fader at -11.2 dB. A male voice, speaking in Hindi, describes Logic Pro X as a powerful digital audio workstation capable of extensive creation and production, noting its current primary use in his workflow, while also mentioning FL Studio for specific sound requirements. As the speaker concludes with a call to action to 'subscribe, like, comment,' and 'follow' for tutorials, a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button appears. The segment transitions to the 'DJ NEO Logo Animation', a white screen displaying a red YouTube play button, the bold red text 'DJ NEO', and the tagline 'ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE', accompanied by a 'SUBSCRIBE FOR MORE VIDEOS LIKE THIS' prompt.

Logic Pro X Interface.description: A dark-themed digital audio workstation interface, explicitly named 'Logic Pro X' by the speaker, who describes it as 'powerful' and his preferred tool for 'most of his production.' It shows two software instrument tracks labeled 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Strings', both with their record enable buttons toggled on. The timeline displays two green MIDI regions, each spanning four bars. The control bar shows time signature 4/4, tempo 120 BPM, and key signature C major. The left panel includes track mixer controls with volume faders and pan settings, and the 'Strings' track is selected with its volume fader at -11.2 dB. The interface remains static with no active editing during the segment, while the speaker discusses its capabilities and his usage.

DJ NEO Logo Animation.description: A white screen with a red YouTube play button icon centered, accompanied by the text 'DJ NEO' in bold red letters. Below it, the tagline 'ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' and the prompt 'SUBSCRIBE FOR MORE VIDEOS LIKE THIS' are displayed in smaller black text. This serves as the outro animation, appearing directly after the speaker's verbal call to action for subscriptions and more tutorials, reinforcing the channel's identity and purpose.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a static view of the Logic Pro X Interface, a dark-themed digital audio workstation displaying 'Yamaha Grand Piano' and 'Strings' software instrument tracks, both record-enabled. Two green MIDI regions, each four bars long, are visible on the timeline, while the control bar shows a 4/4 time signature, 120 BPM tempo, and C major key. The user's cursor hovers, but no active editing occurs. The left panel reveals the track mixer, with the 'Strings' track selected and its volume fader set to -11.2 dB. Throughout this visual, a male voice, speaking in Hindi, provides an overview of Logic Pro X, describing it as a powerful digital audio workstation where "many things can be made and done," and stating that "most of his production happens in Logic Pro these days." He also mentions using FL Studio for specific sound needs. As the speaker concludes by encouraging viewers to "subscribe, like, comment," and "follow" for more tutorials, a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button appears at the bottom right. This is followed by a transition to the 'DJ NEO Logo Animation', a white screen featuring a red YouTube play button, the bold red text 'DJ NEO', and the tagline 'ELECTRONIC MUSIC PRODUCTION ONLINE' with a prompt to 'SUBSCRIBE FOR MORE VIDEOS LIKE THIS', marking the end of the tutorial and the beginning of the outro.

The speaker explicitly names it, saying "Studio Strings" and "Look, here's my Studio Strings" in Hindi, confirming its appearance and function.
